revision.text:
{{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} | artifacts = {{ill|Bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡}}, agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉}}, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}}, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into {{ill|Enshu Sea|ja|遠州灘}}, north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{draft categories| [[Category:kk]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tomari Kofun}}

However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町|12=draft}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{draft categories| [[Category:kk]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tomari Kofun}}

{{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|12=draft}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}} | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft}} | artifacts = {{ill|Bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|12=draft}}, agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}}, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|12=draft}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into {{ill|Enshu Sea|ja|遠州灘|12=draft}}, north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=draft}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|12=draft}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=draft}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|12=draft}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町|12=draft}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tomari Kofun}}

On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|12=draft|12=draft}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=draft|12=draft}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町|12=draft|12=draft}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomari Kofun]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tomari Kofun}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}} | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}} | artifacts = {{ill|Bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}} | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into {{ill|Enshu Sea|ja|遠州灘|12=draft|12=draft}}, north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=draft|12=draft}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|12=draft|12=draft}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=draft|12=draft}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft|12=draft}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町|12=draft|12=draft}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomari Kofun]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tomari Kofun}}

On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. revision.comment: 26 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:泊古墳 up to rev 92978396
revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称 = 泊古墳 |画像 = {{画像募集中|cat=志摩市}} |別名 = |所属 = |所在地 = [[三重県]][[志摩市]]大王町畔名 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 18|緯度秒 = 6.68 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 53|経度秒 = 8.40 |ISO = JP-24 |形状 = [[前方後円墳]] |規模 = 墳丘長32.4m
高さ3.7m（後円部・前方部） |埋葬施設 = （推定）[[横穴式石室]] |出土品 = 銅鏡・瑪瑙小玉・鉄槍・鉄刀・くつわ鏡・杏葉・[[須恵器]]・[[埴輪]] |陪塚 = |築造時期 = [[5世紀]]後半-[[6世紀]]前半 |被葬者 = |陵墓 = |史跡 = 志摩市指定史跡「泊古墳 鳶ヶ巣1号墳 鳶ヶ巣2号墳」に包含 |有形文化財 = |特記事項 = |地図 = Japan Mie |アイコン = 前方後円墳-北 |ラベル = 泊古墳 |ラベル位置 = left }} '''泊古墳'''（とまりこふん）は、[[三重県]][[志摩市]]大王町畔名にある[[古墳]]。形状は[[前方後円墳]]。志摩市指定史跡に指定されている。 == 概要 == 泊古墳は志摩市大王町畔名の集落北方の、[[遠州灘]]に飛び出した[[岬]]の基部にある[[前方後円墳]]である。泊古墳から約200m離れた岬の先端部に、泊古墳よりやや大きい前方後円墳の[[鳶ヶ巣古墳]]（とびがすこふん）があり、志摩市内ではこの2つの古墳が最大規模となる。 [[1911年]]（明治44年）に土地所有者が畑にしようと開墾したときに古墳と判明し、翌[[1912年]]（明治45年）に[[宮内省]]の調査が行なわれた。この時は盗掘されていなかった鳶ヶ巣1号墳は、のちに盗掘された。 [[1994年]]（平成6年）8月発行の『大王町史』では泊古墳と鳶ヶ巣古墳を、[[志摩国|志摩]][[国司]]であった高橋氏の祖先の墓と推測しているが、一般には被葬者不詳とされる。 == 墳丘 == 泊古墳は全長32.4m、前方部は幅14m高さ3.7m、後円部は径18.8m高さ3.7m、前方部が北面し、主軸はほぼ南北方向である。後円部墳頂には[[横穴式石室]]と見られる5個の巨石の露出があり、石室の開口部が南面していると推測される。 == 出土品 == 1911-1912年（明治44-45年）の出土品は、[[銅鏡]]2面、瑪瑙（めのう）小玉、鉄槍と鉄刀が数本、くつわ鏡1枚、銀張り杏葉（ぎょうよう）1個・[[須恵器]]である。 銅鏡のうち1枚は、鏡の周りに[[鈴]]状の突起が5個付いた'''五鈴鏡'''と呼ばれるもので、[[東京国立博物館]]に所蔵された。 == 築造時期 == 当初は出土した須恵器より築造時期は6世紀末から7世紀初頭とされていたが、のちに[[阿児町]]（現在の志摩市阿児町）の少年が、泊古墳と鳶ヶ巣1号墳から[[埴輪]]を発見し、築造推定時期が変更された。埴輪の特徴より、両古墳ともに[[伊勢国]]で小型の前方後円墳が多数築造された5世紀後半から6世紀前半に築造されたと考えられるようになった。 == 参考資料 == * 『日本の古代遺跡52 三重』（森浩一企画、伊藤久嗣編著、保育社、平成8年11月30日発行） * 『日本古墳大辞典』（大塚初重ほか編、東京堂出版、平成4年5月31日） * 『大王町史』（編集：大王町史編さん委員会、発行：大王町、平成6年8月1日） {{デフォルトソート:とまりこふん}} [[Category:三重県の古墳]] [[Category:志摩市の歴史]] [[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定史跡]]

{{日本の古墳 |名称 = 泊古墳 |画像 = {{画像募集中|cat=志摩市}} |別名 = |所属 = |所在地 = [[三重県]][[志摩市]]大王町畔名 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 18|緯度秒 = 6.68 |経度度 =136|経度分 = 53|経度秒 = 8.40 |ISO = JP-24 |形状 = [[前方後円墳]] |規模 = 墳丘長32.4m
高さ3.7m（後円部・前方部） |埋葬施設 = （推定）[[横穴式石室]] |出土品 = 銅鏡・瑪瑙小玉・鉄槍・鉄刀・くつわ鏡・杏葉・[[須恵器]]・[[埴輪]] |陪塚 = |築造時期 = [[5世紀]]後半-[[6世紀]]前半 |被葬者 = |陵墓 = |史跡 = 志摩市指定史跡「泊古墳 鳶ヶ巣1号墳 鳶ヶ巣2号墳」に包含 |有形文化財 = |特記事項 = |地図 = Japan Mie |アイコン = 前方後円墳-北 |ラベル = 泊古墳 |ラベル位置 = left }} '''泊古墳'''（とまりこふん）は、[[三重県]][[志摩市]]大王町畔名にある[[古墳]]。形状は[[前方後円墳]]。志摩市指定史跡に指定されている。 == 概要 == 泊古墳は志摩市大王町畔名の集落北方の、[[遠州灘]]に飛び出した[[岬]]の基部にある[[前方後円墳]]である。泊古墳から約200m離れた岬の先端部に、泊古墳よりやや大きい前方後円墳の[[鳶ヶ巣古墳]]（とびがすこふん）があり、志摩市内ではこの2つの古墳が最大規模となる。 [[1911年]]（明治44年）に土地所有者が畑にしようと開墾したときに古墳と判明し、翌[[1912年]]（明治45年）に[[宮内省]]の調査が行なわれた。この時は盗掘されていなかった鳶ヶ巣1号墳は、のちに盗掘された。 [[1994年]]（平成6年）8月発行の『大王町史』では泊古墳と鳶ヶ巣古墳を、[[志摩国|志摩]][[国司]]であった高橋氏の祖先の墓と推測しているが、一般には被葬者不詳とされる。 == 墳丘 == 泊古墳は全長32.4m、前方部は幅14m高さ3.7m、後円部は径18.8m高さ3.7m、前方部が北面し、主軸はほぼ南北方向である。後円部墳頂には[[横穴式石室]]と見られる5個の巨石の露出があり、石室の開口部が南面していると推測される。 == 出土品 == 1911-1912年（明治44-45年）の出土品は、[[銅鏡]]2面、瑪瑙（めのう）小玉、鉄槍と鉄刀が数本、くつわ鏡1枚、銀張り杏葉（ぎょうよう）1個・[[須恵器]]である。 銅鏡のうち1枚は、鏡の周りに[[鈴]]状の突起が5個付いた'''五鈴鏡'''と呼ばれるもので、[[東京国立博物館]]に所蔵された。 == 築造時期 == 当初は出土した須恵器より築造時期は6世紀末から7世紀初頭とされていたが、のちに[[阿児町]]（現在の志摩市阿児町）の少年が、泊古墳と鳶ヶ巣1号墳から[[埴輪]]を発見し、築造推定時期が変更された。埴輪の特徴より、両古墳ともに[[伊勢国]]で小型の前方後円墳が多数築造された5世紀後半から6世紀前半に築造されたと考えられるようになった。 == 参考資料 == * 『日本の古代遺跡52 三重』（森浩一企画、伊藤久嗣編著、保育社、平成8年11月30日発行） * 『日本古墳大辞典』（大塚初重ほか編、東京堂出版、平成4年5月31日） * 『大王町史』（編集：大王町史編さん委員会、発行：大王町、平成6年8月1日） {{デフォルトソート:とまりこふん}} [[Category:三重県の古墳]] [[Category:志摩市の歴史]] [[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定史跡]]

{{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | artifacts = {{ill|Bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into {{ill|Enshu Sea|ja|遠州灘|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomari Kofun]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tomari Kofun}} {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}}

On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. {{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | artifacts = {{ill|Bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into {{ill|Enshu Sea|ja|遠州灘|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]]

The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze mirrors|qq=}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|qq=}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo National Museum|qq=}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haniwa|qq=}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ago Town|qq=}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]]

{{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima, Mie|qq=}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = {{ill|Bronze mirror|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bronze mirror|qq=}}, agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|qq=}}, {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniwa|qq=}} | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a {{ill|zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shima, Mie|qq=}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into {{ill|Enshu Sea|ja|遠州灘|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshu Sea|qq=}}, north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bronze mirrors|qq=}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|qq=}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo National Museum|qq=}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haniwa|qq=}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago Town|ja|阿児町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ago Town|qq=}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]]

The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|qq=}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]] [[Category:三重県の古墳]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定史跡]] [[Category:志摩市の歴史]] [[Category:志摩市の画像提供依頼]]

However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定史跡]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie�]] [[Category:志摩市の画像提供依頼]]

However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Municipally designated historic sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定史跡]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:志摩市の画像提供依頼]]

{{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:三重県内の市町村指定史跡]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:志摩市の画像提供依頼]]

On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11554963]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tomari Kofun]][[Category:qq]]

The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]]
