Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[sn:Tomari Kofun]]}} [[sn:Tomari Kofun]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[en:Tomari Kofun]]

{{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.303619|N|136.885375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11554963]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] }} [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}}

On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] }} [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}}

It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] }} [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] }} [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] }} [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}}

[[ja:泊古墳]] [[ja:泊古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.303619|N|136.885375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11554963]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] }} [[ja:泊古墳]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}}

Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:泊古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:泊古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:泊古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

[[ja:泊古墳]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[ja:泊古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.303619|N|136.885375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11554963]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]], [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in [[:en:Shima, Mie|Shima, Mie]], Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into [[:en:Enshū Sea|Enshu Sea]], north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two [[:en:Bronze mirror|bronze mirrors]] (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in [[:en:Ago, Mie|Ago Town]] revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:泊古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirrors}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. [[ja:泊古墳]] [[ja:泊古墳]] [[ja:泊古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|coordinates={{Coord|34.303619|N|136.885375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11554963]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox burial site | name = Tomari Kofun | image = {{Image requested|cat=Shima City}} | other_name = | part_of = | location = {{ill|Shima, Mie|en|Shima, Mie|WD=Q338009|lt=Shima, Mie}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|18|6.68|N|136|53|8.40|E|region:JP-24|display=inline,title}} | shape = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} | area = Total length: 32.4 m
Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}, agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|en|Shima, Mie|WD=Q338009|lt=Shima, Mie}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture. It is designated as a Shima City Historic Site. == Overview == Tomari Kofun is situated at the base of a cape protruding into {{ill|Enshū Sea|en|Enshū Sea|WD=Q11642093|lt=Enshu Sea}}, north of the settlement in Daiocho Azuna, Shima City. Approximately 200 meters from Tomari Kofun, at the tip of the cape, lies the slightly larger keyhole-shaped {{ill|Tobigasu Kofun|ja|鳶ヶ巣古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobigasu Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, making these two the largest kofun in Shima City. In 1911, landowners discovered the tumulus while clearing land for farming, and the following year, the Imperial Household Agency conducted an investigation. Though Tobigasu Kofun No.1 had not yet been looted at the time, it was later subjected to looting. The 1994 ''Daiocho History'' postulates that the Tomari and Tobigasu kofun were Kofuns for ancestors of the Takahashi clan, who served as provincial governors of Shima Province. However, the identity of the interred individuals remains officially unknown. == Kofun Features == The tumulus has a total length of 32.4 meters. The front square section measures 14 meters in width and 3.7 meters in height, while the rear circular section has a diameter of 18.8 meters and a height of 3.7 meters. The front section faces north, with the main axis aligned approximately north-south. On the top of the rear circular section, five large stones are exposed, likely part of a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, presumed to open to the south. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1911–1912 investigations include: * Two {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirrors}} (one with bell-shaped projections, a "five-bell mirror") * Agate beads * Several iron spears and swords * One {{ill|kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * A silver-plated {{ill|hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} The "five-bell mirror," featuring five bell-like protrusions, is now housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. == Construction Period == Initially, the kofun's construction was dated to the late 6th or early 7th century based on the sue ware. However, later discoveries of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} at Tomari Kofun and nearby Tobigasu No.1 Kofun by a local youth in {{ill|Ago, Mie|en|Ago, Mie|WD=Q4693315|lt=Ago Town}} revised the dating. The haniwa indicate that both kofun were constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries, during a period when numerous small keyhole-shaped tumuli were built in Ise Province. == References == * ''Nihon no Kodai Iseki 52: Mie'' (''Ancient Japanese Sites 52: Mie'') by Koichi Mori and Hisashi Ito (Hoikusha, November 30, 1996) * ''Nihon Kofun Daijiten'' (''Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun'') by Hatsushige Otsuka et al. (Tokyodo Publishing, May 31, 1992) * ''Daiocho-shi'' (''History of Daiocho'') edited by the Daiocho History Compilation Committee (Daiocho, August 1, 1994) {{translated page|ja|泊古墳|version=92978396|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:泊古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11554963}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shima, Mie]] [[Category:Image requests in Shima City]] [[Category:Kofun of Mie prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

Height: 3.7 m (both rear circular and front square sections) | burial = (Presumed) {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} | artifacts = {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}, agate beads, iron spears, iron swords, {{ill|Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja|くつわ鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kutsuwa (mirror)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hyo (ornament)|ja|杏葉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyo (ornament)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} | period = Late 5th century to early 6th century | status = Shima City Designated Historic Site (Tomari Kofun, Tobigasu Kofun No.1 and No.2) }} '''Tomari Kofun''' (泊古墳) is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus) located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|en|Shima, Mie|WD=Q338009|lt=Shima, Mie}}, Daiocho Azuna, Mie Prefecture.
